findings.description: The large bold poster-style text in the PDF (page 1) is rendered at a much smaller size in the HTML (page 2). The PDF shows the consumer advisory as a large-format sign filling the entire page, while the HTML renders it as standard document text.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 shows 'CONSUMER ADVISORY' in very large bold display text filling the page like a poster. HTML page 2 shows it as a normal-sized heading with body text below.

findings.description: The italicized words 'oysters, clams or mussels' in the PDF poster are rendered in italics in the HTML but the visual emphasis is less prominent due to the smaller text size.
findings.evidence: PDF has large italic text for 'oysters, clams or mussels'; HTML preserves the italic but at standard body text size.

findings.description: The PDF page 1 is a single centered poster layout. The HTML renders it as left-aligned body text with a horizontal rule separator, changing the visual structure from a poster to a document format.
findings.evidence: PDF: centered bold poster-style layout. HTML: left-aligned document with horizontal rule under heading.

findings.description: The HTML combines content from the PDF poster (page 1) and the supplementary text (pages 2-3) into a single continuous flow on pages 2-3, which preserves reading order but changes the visual separation.
findings.evidence: PDF separates the poster advisory and the supplementary regulation text across pages. HTML flows them together on pages 2-3.

findings.description: Nested <main> elements in the HTML structure
findings.evidence: The HTML contains <main> nested inside another <main>, which is a structural issue but does not cause content duplication.
